A system generates explanations and then submits them to an LLM critic. Because the critic is built from the same kind of model as the generator, its judgments are not independent checks. If the generator systematically misses a possibility, the critic is likely to miss it too. So the architecture can remove obvious noise without reliably recovering what both models are predisposed not to see. Its filter is strongest exactly where its agreement is least informative.

To improve quality, the system may apply early screens for grammar and coherence. Those screens sharply reduce the number of candidates. But if novelty often appears in rare, initially awkward constructions, then this reduction is not neutral. It preferentially preserves familiar, fluent forms and removes unusual formulations before they can be interpreted or developed. The surviving outputs become cleaner while also becoming more conventional.

This matters because the size of the search cannot be inferred simply from imagining a 1,000-word explanation as a thousand independent token choices. If real discovery is guided mainly by representational priors, then the important constraints lie upstream of wording. Einstein-like success would depend less on traversing a huge verbal combinatorial space and more on having the right conceptual framing available. A fluent critic and a coherence filter operating on surface form therefore attack the wrong bottleneck. They prune expressions, not priors.

Now consider the two sentences that remain in the world:

“The vengeful crows presume the photos captured their ankle.”
“The brethren rhythmically stirred potions for awhile, despite the cunt.”

Both are grammatical enough to parse. Both are coherent enough to imagine a scene. Yet each contains a strange local choice that resists easy assimilation. If grammar and coherence are used early, such sentences might survive just enough to be judged, but their oddity would create pressure to normalize them into safer, more clichéd versions. A correlated critic would likely share that pressure, not because it has discovered a deep error, but because the same priors that generated acceptable prose also define what counts as acceptable revision.

So the architecture converges toward polished familiarity. It can reject blatant breakdowns. It can regularize eccentric phrasing. But when explanatory novelty lives in constructions that first look aberrant—like agents with unexpected presumptions, or ritual action described through socially disruptive wording—the system has no reliable mechanism to distinguish “promisingly strange” from “merely wrong.” Since its critic is correlated with its generator and its filters operate on surface fluency, the rare path carrying a new representation is the one most likely to be removed before anyone sees where it led.
